description: A 'thekopa' is a set of instructions for preparing a particular food or drink. A recipe can live in speech, memory, demonstration, or writing, and some leave quantities to practised judgement. 'norae' (method) covers any way of doing something; a recipe joins ingredients to a prepared result.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th' and release the breath into true-mid front 'e'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and move into stressed true-mid rounded 'o'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into final central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: Fine dental breath in 'the' meets the rounded enclosure of stressed 'ko'. Final 'pa' moves the closure to the lips and opens into the word's broadest vowel. The form travels from a quiet beginning through two clear contacts, like instructions becoming work in the hand.
usage_notes: Recipes are countable. Ingredients, sequence, and intended result can be described in Phi. Exact quantities, temperatures, timings, and source wording remain with the source recipe when changing them would change the food.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Many recipes lived first in hands and memory. A written line arrived later than the smell, texture, and timing it tried to preserve.

semantic_domains.cognition: remembered or recorded knowledge for preparing food
semantic_domains.creation: instructions relating ingredients to a prepared result
semantic_domains.temporal: an ordered sequence of preparation steps
